attachment.content:
- datalake-ci-permissions: Use when user asks to add or update any AWS resource deployment or GitLab CI IAM roles scoped to the datalake environment. Do not trigger for non-datalake environments (dev, prod, sandbox, services) unless explicitly requested.
- mr-description: Writes merge request message description when raising a GitLab Merge Request. Use when creating a merge request or when user asks to summarise changes for a merge request.
- commit-message: Generate concise git commit messages for Data Engineering changes. Use PROACTIVELY when the user asks to commit files, create a commit, or generate a git commit message.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt:
Explore the codebase at /Users/saurabhtyagi/Documents/workspace/dns-de-rte-orchestrator and produce a comprehensive architecture summary. I need to understand:

1. The overall project structure (directories, key files)
2. What AWS Lambda functions exist and what each does
3. The SAM template (template.yaml) — resources, integrations, event sources
4. The data flow / orchestration logic
5. Key Python modules, classes, and their responsibilities
6. External integrations (AWS services, APIs, databases)
7. What has been implemented vs what appears to be TODO/incomplete

Read the following files at minimum: README.md, TODO.md, template.yaml, pyproject.toml, and the main Python source files. Also list the directory tree. Report back with a structured summary.

content.content:
1	# GDPR Right to Erasure Orchestration Platform
2	
3	[![made-with-python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Made%20with-Python-1f425f.svg)](https://www.python.org/)
4	[![prek](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/j178/prek/master/docs/assets/badge-v0.json)](https://github.com/j178/prek)
5	[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)
6	[![pipeline status](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/pipeline.svg)](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/commits/master)
7	![coverage](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/coverage.svg?job=test)
8	
9	This project was originally created to replace the
10	[Tines](https://www.tines.com/) based Right to Erasure Orchestrator.
11	
12	## Project Goals
13	
14	The primary goal of this project is to replace the existing Tines based
15	orchestrator for subject erasure requests in regards to the GDPR legislation.
16	
17	The main problems being solved are as followed:
18	
19	- The costs related to the use of Tines
20	- The use of Jira as a point of integration and state storage
21	
22	This project also moves the ownership of the orchestration into the Data
23	Engineering team.
24	
25	## Project Details
26	
27	This project is built using Python 3.13+ and uses
28	[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) as a package manager and build tool.
29	
30	> [!note]
31	> If you need help setting up your development environment, please refer to
32	> [docs/setup-local-dev.md](/docs/setup-local-dev.md).
33	
34	## Development
35	
36	After cloning this repository you should run:
37	
38	```sh
39	uv sync --all-groups
40	uv run prek install
41	```
42	
43	This should create a virtual environment under `.venv` in the local directory,
44	download all dependencies, and install a Git pre-commit hook.
45	
46	Generally speaking, if you want to run a normal Python command you should
47	prefix it with `uv run`.
48	
49	### Testing
50	
51	This project uses `pytest` for unit testing. These should be run using the
52	following command:
53	
54	```sh
55	uv run pytest
56	```
57	
58	`pytest-cov` is used as an extension that can help you see how much coverage
59	the tests are actually giving. You can run this as follows:
60	
61	```sh
62	uv run pytest --cov=dns_de_rte_orchestrator
63	```
64	
65	There isn't a set test coverage percentage code should have, but key files
66	should generally have more coverage than others.
67	
68	### Structure
69	
70	The key entry points for AWS Lambda are:
71	
72	- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`
73	- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py`
74	
75	The storage logic sits primarily in the `RequestRepository` parent class and its
76	implementations in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py`.
77	
78	The business logic sits in the `RequestProcessor` class in
79	`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`.
80	
81	### Managing Dependencies
82	
83	`pip` commands should not be used to manage dependencies, instead you should
84	use commands such as:
85	
86	```sh
87	# To add a library dependency
88	uv add library
89	# To add a development dependency only
90	uv add --dev pytest
91	# To remove a dependency
92	uv remove library
93	```
94	
95	This should update the `uv.lock` file, but if in doubt you can run `uv lock`
96	to regenerate it.
97	
98	You can attempt to upgrade packages using the following `uv` command:
99	
100	```sh
101	uv lock --upgrade-package library
102	```
103	
104	## Deployment
105	
106	The code in this project is deployed as 2 separate AWS Lambdas using [AWS
107	SAM](https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/).
108	
109	The versions of Python used during development, testing, and deployment should
110	all match as closely as possible.
111	
112	GitLab runners are used to execute tests and deployments. The configuration for
113	this is managed in `.gitlab-ci.yml`.
114	
115	### SAM
116	
117	AWS SAM uses a custom `Makefile` for building the package that gets uploaded to
118	AWS. This `Makefile` should not be invoked manually. The gist of it is:
119	
120	- It generates a `requirements.txt` with all the non-dev dependencies based on
121	  the `uv.lock` file
122	- It installs all these requirements into the artifact directory
123	- It copies the project `src/` code into the artifact directory
124	- It deletes the `requirements.txt` file
125	
126	The reason for the custom process is to reduce the size of the uploaded
127	artifact and to keep it replicable across different AWS SAM versions.
128	
129	> [!note]
130	> `sam local invoke` has problems when combined with the
131	> [ServerlessAccessManager](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/platform-as-a-service/serverless-applications/serverless-access-manager/)
132	> that the DSO team provide for us to use. You'll need to comment it out and
133	> any lines that reference it in order to get `sam local invoke` to work.
134	
135	To do a manual deployment you'll need to do the following:
136	
137	```sh
138	# First run the build process, this will execute the Makefile
139	sam build
140	# Next deploy
141	sam deploy \
142	  --config-file ../../samconfig.toml \
143	  --config-env dev \
144	  --template-file .aws-sam/build/template.yaml
145	```
146	
147	Note that you'll need to point at the `samconfig.toml` in the project root. By
148	default AWS SAM will try and search the same directory as the template file for
149	the `samconfig.toml`.
150	
151	## Code Quality Tools
152	
153	This project makes use of several tools to ensure consistent code quality.
154	
155	You can run these tools locally alongside `pytest` by running:
156	
157	```sh
158	./local-ci.sh
159	```
160	
161	### prek
162	
163	[prek](https://prek.j178.dev/) is a replacement for the
164	[pre-commit](https://pre-commit.com/) framework. Both allow you to run scripts
165	and checks before a Git commit is created.
166	
167	`prek` is primarily used to run the other code quality tools and ensure that
168	the repository remains in a high quality state.
169	
170	You will need to install the Git pre-commit hooks once after cloning the
171	repository by running:
172	
173	```sh
174	uv run prek install
175	```
176	
177	This should force `prek` to run before all local commits.
178	
179	You can also manually run `prek` by using:
180	
181	```sh
182	uv run prek
183	```
184	
185	The `prek` configuration is stored in `prek.toml`. It configures many
186	pre-commit checks and runs `ruff`, `basedpyright` and `black`.
187	
188	### ruff
189	
190	[ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) is both a fast linter AND code formatter
191	for Python. This project primarily uses it to lint code.
192	
193	You can run it via:
194	
195	```sh
196	uv run ruff check
197	```
198	
199	This can prevent common Python mistakes.
200	
201	### basedpyright
202	
203	[basedpyright](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/) is a fork of `pyright`.
204	It works as a static type-checker for Python code.
205	
206	You should include type hints on as much code as possible to help it out and
207	manage programmer expectations. You should also avoid using deprecated types
208	along with the `Any` type when possible.
209	
210	You can manually run `basedpyright` using `uv`:
211	
212	```sh
213	uv run basedpyright
214	```
215	
216	Sometimes libraries may not include enough type information for `basedpyright`.
217	In these instances you can create type stubs. This process can be done by hand,
218	or you can try to use `basedpyright` to generate said stubs for you via a
219	command like:
220	
221	```sh
222	uv run basedpyright --createstub library
223	```
224	
225	These stubs would be placed in the `typings/` directory at the root of the
226	project.
227	
228	Sometimes type stubs may not be enough, in this case you can add [ignore
229	comments](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/configuration/comments/) to the
230	code.
231	
232	```py
233	# pyright: ignore
234	```
235	
236	You can also edit the `pyrightconfig.json` and add specific libraries to
237	`allowedUntypedLibraries` to allow a whole library to be untyped.
238	
239	#### boto3
240	
241	For `boto3` type stubs there is a tool called
242	[mypy_boto3_builder](https://youtype.github.io/mypy_boto3_builder/) which is
243	used to generate type stubs. This is due to the fact that the `boto3` library
244	is updated frequently, is large, and doesn't include good type information.
245	
246	These stubs can be generated by using `uvx` and `mypy-boto3-builder`, however a
247	script has been provided to keep these up to date called
248	`regen-boto3-type-hints.sh` that will do this for you in a consistent manner.
249	You should use this script when updating the `boto3` version in
250	`pyproject.toml` (and keep it pinned to a specific version) or when you want to
251	add type hints for more AWS services.
252	
253	The type information for `boto3` is stored in the `vendored/` directory.
254	
255	> [!note]
256	> The way Python `.whl` files work means that regenerating them will always
257	> result in a different file as they contain timestamps within them. And the
258	> way `uv.lock` files work is that they store a hash of the `.whl` file. This
259	> is why the `vendored/` file is stored in the repository.
260	
261	When you refer to these types, you should use `TYPE_CHECKING` to guard their
262	inclusion in your code and replace them with stub definitions. For example:
263	
264	```py
265	from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any
266	
267	if TYPE_CHECKING:
268	    from types_boto3_dynamodb.client import DynamoDBClient
269	    from types_boto3_dynamodb.type_defs import (
270	        AttributeValueTypeDef,
271	        UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef,
272	    )
273	else:
274	    DynamoDBClient = Any
275	    AttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
276	    UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
277	```
278	
279	This is because the types are not included when the Python code is packaged and
280	built for AWS SAM deployments.
281	
282	### black
283	
284	[black](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) is a code formatter for
285	Python.
286	
287	You can run `black` directly on a specific file using:
288	
289	```sh
290	uv run black path/to/file.py
291	```
292	
293	`black` is used to ensure all Python code follows the same standard of
294	formatting.

# GDPR Right to Erasure Orchestration Platform

[![made-with-python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Made%20with-Python-1f425f.svg)](https://www.python.org/)
[![prek](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/j178/prek/master/docs/assets/badge-v0.json)](https://github.com/j178/prek)
[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)
[![pipeline status](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/pipeline.svg)](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/commits/master)
![coverage](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/coverage.svg?job=test)

This project was originally created to replace the
[Tines](https://www.tines.com/) based Right to Erasure Orchestrator.

## Project Goals

The primary goal of this project is to replace the existing Tines based
orchestrator for subject erasure requests in regards to the GDPR legislation.

The main problems being solved are as followed:

- The costs related to the use of Tines
- The use of Jira as a point of integration and state storage

This project also moves the ownership of the orchestration into the Data
Engineering team.

## Project Details

This project is built using Python 3.13+ and uses
[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) as a package manager and build tool.

> [!note]
> If you need help setting up your development environment, please refer to
> [docs/setup-local-dev.md](/docs/setup-local-dev.md).

## Development

After cloning this repository you should run:

```sh
uv sync --all-groups
uv run prek install
```

This should create a virtual environment under `.venv` in the local directory,
download all dependencies, and install a Git pre-commit hook.

Generally speaking, if you want to run a normal Python command you should
prefix it with `uv run`.

### Testing

This project uses `pytest` for unit testing. These should be run using the
following command:

```sh
uv run pytest
```

`pytest-cov` is used as an extension that can help you see how much coverage
the tests are actually giving. You can run this as follows:

```sh
uv run pytest --cov=dns_de_rte_orchestrator
```

There isn't a set test coverage percentage code should have, but key files
should generally have more coverage than others.

### Structure

The key entry points for AWS Lambda are:

- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py`

The storage logic sits primarily in the `RequestRepository` parent class and its
implementations in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py`.

The business logic sits in the `RequestProcessor` class in
`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`.

### Managing Dependencies

`pip` commands should not be used to manage dependencies, instead you should
use commands such as:

```sh
# To add a library dependency
uv add library
# To add a development dependency only
uv add --dev pytest
# To remove a dependency
uv remove library
```

This should update the `uv.lock` file, but if in doubt you can run `uv lock`
to regenerate it.

You can attempt to upgrade packages using the following `uv` command:

```sh
uv lock --upgrade-package library
```

## Deployment

The code in this project is deployed as 2 separate AWS Lambdas using [AWS
SAM](https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/).

The versions of Python used during development, testing, and deployment should
all match as closely as possible.

GitLab runners are used to execute tests and deployments. The configuration for
this is managed in `.gitlab-ci.yml`.

### SAM

AWS SAM uses a custom `Makefile` for building the package that gets uploaded to
AWS. This `Makefile` should not be invoked manually. The gist of it is:

- It generates a `requirements.txt` with all the non-dev dependencies based on
  the `uv.lock` file
- It installs all these requirements into the artifact directory
- It copies the project `src/` code into the artifact directory
- It deletes the `requirements.txt` file

The reason for the custom process is to reduce the size of the uploaded
artifact and to keep it replicable across different AWS SAM versions.

> [!note]
> `sam local invoke` has problems when combined with the
> [ServerlessAccessManager](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/platform-as-a-service/serverless-applications/serverless-access-manager/)
> that the DSO team provide for us to use. You'll need to comment it out and
> any lines that reference it in order to get `sam local invoke` to work.

To do a manual deployment you'll need to do the following:

```sh
# First run the build process, this will execute the Makefile
sam build
# Next deploy
sam deploy \
  --config-file ../../samconfig.toml \
  --config-env dev \
  --template-file .aws-sam/build/template.yaml
```

Note that you'll need to point at the `samconfig.toml` in the project root. By
default AWS SAM will try and search the same directory as the template file for
the `samconfig.toml`.

## Code Quality Tools

This project makes use of several tools to ensure consistent code quality.

You can run these tools locally alongside `pytest` by running:

```sh
./local-ci.sh
```

### prek

[prek](https://prek.j178.dev/) is a replacement for the
[pre-commit](https://pre-commit.com/) framework. Both allow you to run scripts
and checks before a Git commit is created.

`prek` is primarily used to run the other code quality tools and ensure that
the repository remains in a high quality state.

You will need to install the Git pre-commit hooks once after cloning the
repository by running:

```sh
uv run prek install
```

This should force `prek` to run before all local commits.

You can also manually run `prek` by using:

```sh
uv run prek
```

The `prek` configuration is stored in `prek.toml`. It configures many
pre-commit checks and runs `ruff`, `basedpyright` and `black`.

### ruff

[ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) is both a fast linter AND code formatter
for Python. This project primarily uses it to lint code.

You can run it via:

```sh
uv run ruff check
```

This can prevent common Python mistakes.

### basedpyright

[basedpyright](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/) is a fork of `pyright`.
It works as a static type-checker for Python code.

You should include type hints on as much code as possible to help it out and
manage programmer expectations. You should also avoid using deprecated types
along with the `Any` type when possible.

You can manually run `basedpyright` using `uv`:

```sh
uv run basedpyright
```

Sometimes libraries may not include enough type information for `basedpyright`.
In these instances you can create type stubs. This process can be done by hand,
or you can try to use `basedpyright` to generate said stubs for you via a
command like:

```sh
uv run basedpyright --createstub library
```

These stubs would be placed in the `typings/` directory at the root of the
project.

Sometimes type stubs may not be enough, in this case you can add [ignore
comments](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/configuration/comments/) to the
code.

```py
# pyright: ignore
```

You can also edit the `pyrightconfig.json` and add specific libraries to
`allowedUntypedLibraries` to allow a whole library to be untyped.

#### boto3

For `boto3` type stubs there is a tool called
[mypy_boto3_builder](https://youtype.github.io/mypy_boto3_builder/) which is
used to generate type stubs. This is due to the fact that the `boto3` library
is updated frequently, is large, and doesn't include good type information.

These stubs can be generated by using `uvx` and `mypy-boto3-builder`, however a
script has been provided to keep these up to date called
`regen-boto3-type-hints.sh` that will do this for you in a consistent manner.
You should use this script when updating the `boto3` version in
`pyproject.toml` (and keep it pinned to a specific version) or when you want to
add type hints for more AWS services.

The type information for `boto3` is stored in the `vendored/` directory.

> [!note]
> The way Python `.whl` files work means that regenerating them will always
> result in a different file as they contain timestamps within them. And the
> way `uv.lock` files work is that they store a hash of the `.whl` file. This
> is why the `vendored/` file is stored in the repository.

When you refer to these types, you should use `TYPE_CHECKING` to guard their
inclusion in your code and replace them with stub definitions. For example:

```py
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from types_boto3_dynamodb.client import DynamoDBClient
    from types_boto3_dynamodb.type_defs import (
        AttributeValueTypeDef,
        UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef,
    )
else:
    DynamoDBClient = Any
    AttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
    UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
```

This is because the types are not included when the Python code is packaged and
built for AWS SAM deployments.

### black

[black](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) is a code formatter for
Python.

You can run `black` directly on a specific file using:

```sh
uv run black path/to/file.py
```

`black` is used to ensure all Python code follows the same standard of
formatting.

- [x] Make sure AWS SAM deployment can be done from CI/CD to `dev` and `prod`
  - [x] Confirm AWS SAM deployment can be done to `dev` via CI/CD
  - [x] Confirm AWS SAM deployment can be done to `prod` via CI/CD

1	AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
2	Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
3	Description: >
4	  Right to Erasure Orchestrator.
5	  Originally a replacement for the Tines Orchestrator, this stack primarily
6	  consists of 2 AWS Lambdas that communicate with DynamoDB
7	
8	Parameters:
9	  Environment:
10	    Description: Environment
11	    Type: String
12	  DynamoDBTable:
13	    Description: The DynamoDB table to create new RTE requests in
14	    Type: String
15	  DynamoDBTableGSI:
16	    Description: The DynamoDB table GSI to query for RTE requests
17	    Type: String
18	  AuditLogDynamoDBTable:
19	    Description: The DynamoDB table to send audit logs to
20	    Type: String
21	  VPCEndpointId:
22	    Description: VPC Endpoint ID allowed to invoke the API
23	    Type: String
24	  DryRunMode:
25	    Description: Whether to deploy in Dry Run mode or not
26	    Type: String
27	
28	Resources:
29	  CreateServerlessAccessManager:
30	    Type: Custom::ServerlessAccessManager
31	    Properties:
32	      ServiceToken: !Sub "arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:${AWS::AccountId}:function:paas-serverless-access-manager"
33	      FunctionName: !Sub 'data-rte-orchestrator-create-request-${Environment}'
34	      VpcExecution: "true"
35	      DatadogLogging: "true"
36	      Targets:
37	        DynamoDBTable:
38	          Type: DynamoDB
39	          Properties:
40	            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${DynamoDBTable}"
41	        AuditLogDynamoDBTable:
42	          Type: DynamoDB
43	          Properties:
44	            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${AuditLogDynamoDBTable}"
45	
46	  CreateRequestApi:
47	    Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
48	    Properties:
49	      StageName: !Ref Environment
50	      OpenApiVersion: '2.0'
51	      Description: !Sub "RTE orchestrator create-request API (${Environment})"
52	      EndpointConfiguration:
53	        Type: PRIVATE
54	        VPCEndpointIds:
55	          - !Ref VPCEndpointId
56	      Auth:
57	        ApiKeyRequired: true
58	        ResourcePolicy:
59	          CustomStatements:
60	            - Effect: Allow
61	              Action: execute-api:Invoke
62	              Resource:
63	                - !Sub "arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:*"
64	              Principal: '*'
65	              Condition:
66	                StringEquals:
67	                  aws:SourceVpce: !Ref VPCEndpointId
68	
69	  CreateFunction:
70	    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
71	    Metadata:
72	      BuildMethod: makefile
73	    Properties:
74	      FunctionName: !GetAtt CreateServerlessAccessManager.FunctionName
75	      CodeUri: ./
76	      Handler: dns_de_rte_orchestrator.creator_lambda.lambda_handler
77	      Runtime: python3.13
78	      Tracing: Active
79	      Description: Create RTE requests
80	      MemorySize: 256
81	      Timeout: 30
82	      ReservedConcurrentExecutions: 10
83	      VpcConfig: !GetAtt CreateServerlessAccessManager.VpcConfig
84	      Role: !GetAtt CreateServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleArn
85	      Environment:
86	        Variables:
87	          REPOSITORY_TYPE: "dynamodb"
88	          DYNAMO_DB_TABLE: !Ref DynamoDBTable
89	          DYNAMO_DB_INDEX: !Ref DynamoDBTableGSI
90	          DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE: !Ref AuditLogDynamoDBTable
91	          DRY_RUN_MODE: !Ref DryRunMode
92	          POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME: !Sub "create-erasure-request-${Environment}"
93	          # POWERTOOLS_LOGGER_LOG_EVENT must NOT be set — the incoming event
94	          # contains erasure_details (PII: email, name, postcode), which
95	          # should not be persisted in CloudWatch logs.
96	      Events:
97	        CreateErasureRequest:
98	          Type: Api
99	          Properties:
100	            RestApiId: !Ref CreateRequestApi
101	            Path: /erasure-request
102	            Method: post
103	            Auth:
104	              ApiKeyRequired: true
105	
106	  CreateRequestApiKey:
107	    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::ApiKey
108	    # noinspection YamlUnresolvedReferences
109	    DependsOn: CreateRequestApiStage
110	    Properties:
111	      Name: !Sub "rte-orchestrator-api-key-${Environment}"
112	      Description: !Sub "API key for OneTrust to invoke the RTE create-request API (${Environment})"
113	      Enabled: true
114	
115	  CreateRequestUsagePlan:
116	    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlan
117	    # noinspection YamlUnresolvedReferences
118	    DependsOn: CreateRequestApiStage

120	      UsagePlanName: !Sub "rte-orchestrator-usage-plan-${Environment}"
121	      Description: !Sub "Usage plan for the RTE create-request API (${Environment})"
122	      ApiStages:
123	        - ApiId: !Ref CreateRequestApi
124	          Stage: !Ref Environment
125	
126	  CreateRequestUsagePlanKey:
127	    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlanKey
128	    Properties:
129	      KeyId: !Ref CreateRequestApiKey
130	      KeyType: API_KEY
131	      UsagePlanId: !Ref CreateRequestUsagePlan
132	
133	  ProcessServerlessAccessManager:
134	    Type: Custom::ServerlessAccessManager
135	    Properties:
136	      ServiceToken: !Sub "arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:${AWS::AccountId}:function:paas-serverless-access-manager"
137	      FunctionName: !Sub 'data-rte-orchestrator-process-requests-${Environment}'
138	      VpcExecution: "true"
139	      DatadogLogging: "true"
140	      Targets:
141	        DynamoDBTable:
142	          Type: DynamoDB
143	          Properties:
144	            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${DynamoDBTable}"
145	        AuditLogDynamoDBTable:
146	          Type: DynamoDB
147	          Properties:
148	            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${AuditLogDynamoDBTable}"
149	        DynamoDBIndex:
150	          Type: DynamoDB
151	          Properties:
152	            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${DynamoDBTable}/index/${DynamoDBTableGSI}"
153	
154	  ProcessFunction:
155	    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
156	    Metadata:
157	      BuildMethod: makefile
158	    Properties:
159	      FunctionName: !GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.FunctionName
160	      CodeUri: ./
161	      Handler: dns_de_rte_orchestrator.processor_lambda.lambda_handler
162	      Runtime: python3.13
163	      Tracing: Active
164	      Description: Process RTE requests
165	      MemorySize: 512
166	      Timeout: 900
167	      ReservedConcurrentExecutions: 1
168	      VpcConfig: !GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.VpcConfig
169	      Role: !GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleArn
170	      Environment:
171	        Variables:
172	          REPOSITORY_TYPE: "dynamodb"
173	          DYNAMO_DB_TABLE: !Ref DynamoDBTable
174	          DYNAMO_DB_INDEX: !Ref DynamoDBTableGSI
175	          DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE: !Ref AuditLogDynamoDBTable
176	          PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS: "5000"
177	          DRY_RUN_MODE: !Ref DryRunMode
178	          CUSTOMER_API_URL: !Sub "https://customer-api-proxy-internal-eks.eu-west-1.${Environment}.hbi.systems/v1/customer/find"
179	          POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME: !Sub "process-erasure-requests-${Environment}"
180	          # POWERTOOLS_LOGGER_LOG_EVENT must NOT be set — the incoming event
181	          # contains erasure_details (PII: email, name, postcode), which
182	          # should not be persisted in CloudWatch logs.

file.content:
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Description: >
  Right to Erasure Orchestrator.
  Originally a replacement for the Tines Orchestrator, this stack primarily
  consists of 2 AWS Lambdas that communicate with DynamoDB

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Description: Environment
    Type: String
  DynamoDBTable:
    Description: The DynamoDB table to create new RTE requests in
    Type: String
  DynamoDBTableGSI:
    Description: The DynamoDB table GSI to query for RTE requests
    Type: String
  AuditLogDynamoDBTable:
    Description: The DynamoDB table to send audit logs to
    Type: String
  VPCEndpointId:
    Description: VPC Endpoint ID allowed to invoke the API
    Type: String
  DryRunMode:
    Description: Whether to deploy in Dry Run mode or not
    Type: String

Resources:
  CreateServerlessAccessManager:
    Type: Custom::ServerlessAccessManager
    Properties:
      ServiceToken: !Sub "arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:${AWS::AccountId}:function:paas-serverless-access-manager"
      FunctionName: !Sub 'data-rte-orchestrator-create-request-${Environment}'
      VpcExecution: "true"
      DatadogLogging: "true"
      Targets:
        DynamoDBTable:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${DynamoDBTable}"
        AuditLogDynamoDBTable:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${AuditLogDynamoDBTable}"

  CreateRequestApi:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
    Properties:
      StageName: !Ref Environment
      OpenApiVersion: '2.0'
      Description: !Sub "RTE orchestrator create-request API (${Environment})"
      EndpointConfiguration:
        Type: PRIVATE
        VPCEndpointIds:
          - !Ref VPCEndpointId
      Auth:
        ApiKeyRequired: true
        ResourcePolicy:
          CustomStatements:
            - Effect: Allow
              Action: execute-api:Invoke
              Resource:
                - !Sub "arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:*"
              Principal: '*'
              Condition:
                StringEquals:
                  aws:SourceVpce: !Ref VPCEndpointId

  CreateFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Metadata:
      BuildMethod: makefile
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !GetAtt CreateServerlessAccessManager.FunctionName
      CodeUri: ./
      Handler: dns_de_rte_orchestrator.creator_lambda.lambda_handler
      Runtime: python3.13
      Tracing: Active
      Description: Create RTE requests
      MemorySize: 256
      Timeout: 30
      ReservedConcurrentExecutions: 10
      VpcConfig: !GetAtt CreateServerlessAccessManager.VpcConfig
      Role: !GetAtt CreateServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleArn
      Environment:
        Variables:
          REPOSITORY_TYPE: "dynamodb"
          DYNAMO_DB_TABLE: !Ref DynamoDBTable
          DYNAMO_DB_INDEX: !Ref DynamoDBTableGSI
          DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE: !Ref AuditLogDynamoDBTable
          DRY_RUN_MODE: !Ref DryRunMode
          POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME: !Sub "create-erasure-request-${Environment}"
          # POWERTOOLS_LOGGER_LOG_EVENT must NOT be set — the incoming event
          # contains erasure_details (PII: email, name, postcode), which
          # should not be persisted in CloudWatch logs.
      Events:
        CreateErasureRequest:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            RestApiId: !Ref CreateRequestApi
            Path: /erasure-request
            Method: post
            Auth:
              ApiKeyRequired: true

  CreateRequestApiKey:
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::ApiKey
    # noinspection YamlUnresolvedReferences
    DependsOn: CreateRequestApiStage
    Properties:
      Name: !Sub "rte-orchestrator-api-key-${Environment}"
      Description: !Sub "API key for OneTrust to invoke the RTE create-request API (${Environment})"
      Enabled: true

  CreateRequestUsagePlan:
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlan
    # noinspection YamlUnresolvedReferences
    DependsOn: CreateRequestApiStage
    Properties:
      UsagePlanName: !Sub "rte-orchestrator-usage-plan-${Environment}"
      Description: !Sub "Usage plan for the RTE create-request API (${Environment})"
      ApiStages:
        - ApiId: !Ref CreateRequestApi
          Stage: !Ref Environment

  CreateRequestUsagePlanKey:
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlanKey
    Properties:
      KeyId: !Ref CreateRequestApiKey
      KeyType: API_KEY
      UsagePlanId: !Ref CreateRequestUsagePlan

  ProcessServerlessAccessManager:
    Type: Custom::ServerlessAccessManager
    Properties:
      ServiceToken: !Sub "arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:${AWS::AccountId}:function:paas-serverless-access-manager"
      FunctionName: !Sub 'data-rte-orchestrator-process-requests-${Environment}'
      VpcExecution: "true"
      DatadogLogging: "true"
      Targets:
        DynamoDBTable:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${DynamoDBTable}"
        AuditLogDynamoDBTable:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${AuditLogDynamoDBTable}"
        DynamoDBIndex:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Table: !Sub "arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${DynamoDBTable}/index/${DynamoDBTableGSI}"

  ProcessFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Metadata:
      BuildMethod: makefile
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.FunctionName
      CodeUri: ./
      Handler: dns_de_rte_orchestrator.processor_lambda.lambda_handler
      Runtime: python3.13
      Tracing: Active
      Description: Process RTE requests
      MemorySize: 512
      Timeout: 900
      ReservedConcurrentExecutions: 1
      VpcConfig: !GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.VpcConfig
      Role: !GetAtt ProcessServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleArn
      Environment:
        Variables:
          REPOSITORY_TYPE: "dynamodb"
          DYNAMO_DB_TABLE: !Ref DynamoDBTable
          DYNAMO_DB_INDEX: !Ref DynamoDBTableGSI
          DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE: !Ref AuditLogDynamoDBTable
          PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS: "5000"
          DRY_RUN_MODE: !Ref DryRunMode
          CUSTOMER_API_URL: !Sub "https://customer-api-proxy-internal-eks.eu-west-1.${Environment}.hbi.systems/v1/customer/find"
          POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME: !Sub "process-erasure-requests-${Environment}"
          # POWERTOOLS_LOGGER_LOG_EVENT must NOT be set — the incoming event
          # contains erasure_details (PII: email, name, postcode), which
          # should not be persisted in CloudWatch logs.

Outputs:
  CreateRequestApiInvokeUrl:
    Description: Invoke URL for the RTE create-request API
    Value: !Sub "https://${CreateRequestApi}.execute-api.${AWS::Region}.amazonaws.com/${Environment}/erasure-request"
  CreateRequestApiKeyId:
    Description: ID of the API key required to invoke the RTE create-request API (retrieve value from API Gateway console)
    Value: !Ref CreateRequestApiKey

content.content:
1	import logging
2	from abc import ABC
3	from datetime import datetime
4	from typing import Final
5	
6	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.as400 import AS400Notifier
7	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging import AuditLogger
8	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.customer_api_client import (
9	    ALL_SITE_IDS,
10	    CustomerAPIClient,
11	    CustomerDetails,
12	)
13	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import (
14	    VALID_STATES,
15	    ErasureRequest,
16	    ErasureState,
17	)
18	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_repository import RequestRepository
19	
20	logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
21	
22	
23	class RequestProcessor:
24	    """Container for Erasure Request processing logic"""
25	
26	    PROCESS_STATES: tuple[ErasureState, ...] = (
27	        "new",
28	        "in-progress",
29	        "phase-1",
30	        "phase-2",
31	        "phase-3",
32	    )
33	    """The list of states (in order) that will have items processed"""
34	
35	    def __init__(
36	        self,
37	        repository: RequestRepository,
38	        audit: AuditLogger,
39	        as400: AS400Notifier,
40	        customer_api: CustomerAPIClient,
41	        dry_run: bool = False,
42	    ) -> None:
43	        self.repo: RequestRepository = repository
44	        self.audit: AuditLogger = audit
45	        self.as400: AS400Notifier = as400
46	        self.dry_run: Final[bool] = dry_run
47	        self.customer_api: CustomerAPIClient = customer_api
48	        if dry_run:
49	            logger.warning("Request Processor is in dry-run mode")
50	
51	    def _check_timeout(self, timeout: datetime | None = None) -> bool:
52	        if timeout is None:
53	            return False
54	        now: datetime = datetime.now()
55	        return now >= timeout
56	
57	    def process(self, timeout: datetime | None = None):
58	        """
59	        Process the requests with an optional timeout to bail early before.
60	
61	        When working in a time limited environment like AWS Lambda, this
62	        timeout should be set a little before the whole Lambda will expire to
63	        account for interrupting the running logic.
64	
65	        The only time an error should be raised related to the timeout is if we
66	        timeout before the process has even begun as this indicates too short a
67	        timeout.
68	        """
69	
70	        if timeout is not None:
71	            logger.info("Process triggered with timeout of %s", timeout.isoformat())
72	        else:
73	            logger.info("Process triggered without timeout")
74	
75	        # This should do the following:
76	        # - Get all requests in a non-terminal state
77	        # - process each request appropriatelty
78	        # It should do this for each different non-terminal state in turn and
79	        # be checking for time remaining in-between each to make sure that we
80	        # have enough time left for this invocation, since this could be running
81	        # in an AWS Lambda.
82	        # We can also happily continue at the next function call since we will
83	        # be running often
84	
85	        if self._check_timeout(timeout):
86	            # Timed out before we began
87	            # This isn't right so raise an error
88	            raise ValueError("We timed out before we began")
89	
90	        # NOTE: We could improve the timeout logic by timning each previous
91	        # process. We'd then use this data to estimate if we'd timeout rather
92	        # than using a safety margin
93	
94	        # Collect some stats on the process
95	        failed_request_count: int = 0
96	        successful_request_count: int = 0
97	        processed_request_count: int = 0
98	        timed_out: bool = False
99	
100	        # NOTE: When we get all those requests in a given state, we may
101	        # well end up processing the same request through multiple states
102	        # if it has been transitioned to the next AND the storage layer has
103	        # consistently updated.
104	        for state in self.PROCESS_STATES:
105	            # Stop early if we've timed out
106	            # We can process this state in another iteration
107	            if self._check_timeout(timeout):
108	                logger.info('Timed out before checking state "%s"', state)
109	                timed_out = True
110	                break
111	
112	            all_in_state = self.repo.get_all_in_state(state)
113	            logger.info('Found %d entries for state "%s"', len(all_in_state), state)
114	
115	            for request in all_in_state:
116	                # Stop processing the items if we've timed out
117	                # We can pick back up in the next iteration
118	                if self._check_timeout(timeout):
119	                    logger.info(
120	                        'Timed out while checking state "%s" before request: %s',
121	                        state,
122	                        request,
123	                    )
124	                    timed_out = True
125	                    break
126	                # Process the single erasure request
127	                # We aren't using batch operations here so that we can be
128	                # idempotent as it relates to each individual request
129	                processed_request_count += 1
130	                try:
131	                    # We do this in a try block as we don't want the whole
132	                    # process to fail if individual requests do
133	                    self.process_request(request)
134	                    successful_request_count += 1
135	                except ErasureRequestException:
136	                    logger.exception("Problem processing request %s", request.id)
137	                    failed_request_count += 1
138	
139	        # After all the processing has been completed we need to quickly do
140	        # alerts as we may have timed out
141	        if processed_request_count > 0:
142	            logger.info(
143	                "Processed %d/%d requests successfully",
144	                successful_request_count,
145	                processed_request_count,
146	            )
147	            if failed_request_count >= processed_request_count:
148	                # Raise an exception if all processing failed.
149	                # This could be due to some misconfiguration so the calling
150	                # code should be alerted
151	                raise AllProcessingFailed(failed_request_count)
152	            if timed_out:
153	                # This logging message is partly redundent but if we often
154	                # time out it might be a bad sign
155	                logger.warning("Timed out while processing")
156	            if failed_request_count > 0:
157	                logger.error("%d requests failed to process", failed_request_count)
158	        else:
159	            logger.info("No requests processed")
160	            if timed_out:
161	                logger.warning("We timed out while checking states without finding any")
162	
163	    def process_request(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
164	        """Process a single request"""
165	        # This is where the meat of the business logic should be
166	        logger.info("Processing request: %s", request)
167	        self.audit.log(request.id, "Request was selected for processing")
168	
169	        # Defensively align the state to a standard lower case format
170	        request_state: str = request.state.strip().lower()
171	        if request_state not in VALID_STATES:
172	            raise InvalidRequestState(request)
173	
174	        cast_request_state: ErasureState = request_state
175	        match cast_request_state:
176	            case "new":
177	                self.process_new(request)
178	            case "in-progress":
179	                self.process_in_progress(request)
180	            case "phase-1":
181	                self.process_phase1(request)
182	            case "phase-2":
183	                self.process_phase2(request)
184	            case "phase-3":
185	                self.process_phase3(request)
186	            case _:
187	                raise NotImplementedError(
188	                    f'Request processing of state "{request_state}" not implemented'
189	                )
190	
191	    @classmethod
192	    def _validate_request(
193	        cls, current_state: ErasureState, request: ErasureRequest
194	    ) -> None:
195	        """
196	        Validate a request for the given current state.
197	
198	        This will raise an exception if the request is not valid.
199	        """
200	        if request.state != current_state:
201	            raise WrongStateForProcess(request, current_state)
202	
203	    def _transition_request(
204	        self,
205	        request: ErasureRequest,
206	        new_state: ErasureState,
207	        reason: str | None = None,
208	    ) -> None:
209	        """
210	        Helper function to transition a request to a new state.
211	
212	        You can provide an optional reason that will be used in audit logging.
213	        """
214	        logger.info("Transitioning request %s to %s", request.id, new_state)
215	        request.state = new_state
216	        if self.dry_run:
217	            logger.info("DryRun: Would update repository")
218	        else:
219	            self.repo.update(request)
220	        message = f"Request was transitioned to {new_state}"
221	        if reason:
222	            message = message = " due to: " + reason
223	        if not self.dry_run:
224	            self.audit.log(request.id, message)
225	
226	    def process_new(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
227	        self._validate_request("new", request)
228	        logger.info("Processing new request %s", request.id)
229	
230	        # Check if there is an email attached to the request
231	        email: str | None = request.erasure_details.get("email")
232	
233	        if email is None or email.strip() == "":
234	            # Missing email address, this should always be present as the data
235	            # privacy team verify this in OneTrust before the request reaches
236	            # our system
237	
238	            logger.warning(
239	                "Request %s has no email so will be transitioned to need-more-detail",
240	                request.id,
241	            )
242	            # Update the request before raising an exception

import logging
from abc import ABC
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Final

from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.as400 import AS400Notifier
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging import AuditLogger
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.customer_api_client import (
    ALL_SITE_IDS,
    CustomerAPIClient,
    CustomerDetails,
)
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import (
    VALID_STATES,
    ErasureRequest,
    ErasureState,
)
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_repository import RequestRepository

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class RequestProcessor:
    """Container for Erasure Request processing logic"""

    PROCESS_STATES: tuple[ErasureState, ...] = (
        "new",
        "in-progress",
        "phase-1",
        "phase-2",
        "phase-3",
    )
    """The list of states (in order) that will have items processed"""

    def __init__(
        self,
        repository: RequestRepository,
        audit: AuditLogger,
        as400: AS400Notifier,
        customer_api: CustomerAPIClient,
        dry_run: bool = False,
    ) -> None:
        self.repo: RequestRepository = repository
        self.audit: AuditLogger = audit
        self.as400: AS400Notifier = as400
        self.dry_run: Final[bool] = dry_run
        self.customer_api: CustomerAPIClient = customer_api
        if dry_run:
            logger.warning("Request Processor is in dry-run mode")

    def _check_timeout(self, timeout: datetime | None = None) -> bool:
        if timeout is None:
            return False
        now: datetime = datetime.now()
        return now >= timeout

    def process(self, timeout: datetime | None = None):
        """
        Process the requests with an optional timeout to bail early before.

        When working in a time limited environment like AWS Lambda, this
        timeout should be set a little before the whole Lambda will expire to
        account for interrupting the running logic.

        The only time an error should be raised related to the timeout is if we
        timeout before the process has even begun as this indicates too short a
        timeout.
        """

        if timeout is not None:
            logger.info("Process triggered with timeout of %s", timeout.isoformat())
        else:
            logger.info("Process triggered without timeout")

        # This should do the following:
        # - Get all requests in a non-terminal state
        # - process each request appropriatelty
        # It should do this for each different non-terminal state in turn and
        # be checking for time remaining in-between each to make sure that we
        # have enough time left for this invocation, since this could be running
        # in an AWS Lambda.
        # We can also happily continue at the next function call since we will
        # be running often

        if self._check_timeout(timeout):
            # Timed out before we began
            # This isn't right so raise an error
            raise ValueError("We timed out before we began")

        # NOTE: We could improve the timeout logic by timning each previous
        # process. We'd then use this data to estimate if we'd timeout rather
        # than using a safety margin

        # Collect some stats on the process
        failed_request_count: int = 0
        successful_request_count: int = 0
        processed_request_count: int = 0
        timed_out: bool = False

        # NOTE: When we get all those requests in a given state, we may
        # well end up processing the same request through multiple states
        # if it has been transitioned to the next AND the storage layer has
        # consistently updated.
        for state in self.PROCESS_STATES:
            # Stop early if we've timed out
            # We can process this state in another iteration
            if self._check_timeout(timeout):
                logger.info('Timed out before checking state "%s"', state)
                timed_out = True
                break

            all_in_state = self.repo.get_all_in_state(state)
            logger.info('Found %d entries for state "%s"', len(all_in_state), state)

            for request in all_in_state:
                # Stop processing the items if we've timed out
                # We can pick back up in the next iteration
                if self._check_timeout(timeout):
                    logger.info(
                        'Timed out while checking state "%s" before request: %s',
                        state,
                        request,
                    )
                    timed_out = True
                    break
                # Process the single erasure request
                # We aren't using batch operations here so that we can be
                # idempotent as it relates to each individual request
                processed_request_count += 1
                try:
                    # We do this in a try block as we don't want the whole
                    # process to fail if individual requests do
                    self.process_request(request)
                    successful_request_count += 1
                except ErasureRequestException:
                    logger.exception("Problem processing request %s", request.id)
                    failed_request_count += 1

        # After all the processing has been completed we need to quickly do
        # alerts as we may have timed out
        if processed_request_count > 0:
            logger.info(
                "Processed %d/%d requests successfully",
                successful_request_count,
                processed_request_count,
            )
            if failed_request_count >= processed_request_count:
                # Raise an exception if all processing failed.
                # This could be due to some misconfiguration so the calling
                # code should be alerted
                raise AllProcessingFailed(failed_request_count)
            if timed_out:
                # This logging message is partly redundent but if we often
                # time out it might be a bad sign
                logger.warning("Timed out while processing")
            if failed_request_count > 0:
                logger.error("%d requests failed to process", failed_request_count)
        else:
            logger.info("No requests processed")
            if timed_out:
                logger.warning("We timed out while checking states without finding any")

    def process_request(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        """Process a single request"""
        # This is where the meat of the business logic should be
        logger.info("Processing request: %s", request)
        self.audit.log(request.id, "Request was selected for processing")

        # Defensively align the state to a standard lower case format
        request_state: str = request.state.strip().lower()
        if request_state not in VALID_STATES:
            raise InvalidRequestState(request)

        cast_request_state: ErasureState = request_state
        match cast_request_state:
            case "new":
                self.process_new(request)
            case "in-progress":
                self.process_in_progress(request)
            case "phase-1":
                self.process_phase1(request)
            case "phase-2":
                self.process_phase2(request)
            case "phase-3":
                self.process_phase3(request)
            case _:
                raise NotImplementedError(
                    f'Request processing of state "{request_state}" not implemented'
                )

    @classmethod
    def _validate_request(
        cls, current_state: ErasureState, request: ErasureRequest
    ) -> None:
        """
        Validate a request for the given current state.

        This will raise an exception if the request is not valid.
        """
        if request.state != current_state:
            raise WrongStateForProcess(request, current_state)

    def _transition_request(
        self,
        request: ErasureRequest,
        new_state: ErasureState,
        reason: str | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        """
        Helper function to transition a request to a new state.

        You can provide an optional reason that will be used in audit logging.
        """
        logger.info("Transitioning request %s to %s", request.id, new_state)
        request.state = new_state
        if self.dry_run:
            logger.info("DryRun: Would update repository")
        else:
            self.repo.update(request)
        message = f"Request was transitioned to {new_state}"
        if reason:
            message = message = " due to: " + reason
        if not self.dry_run:
            self.audit.log(request.id, message)

    def process_new(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self._validate_request("new", request)
        logger.info("Processing new request %s", request.id)

        # Check if there is an email attached to the request
        email: str | None = request.erasure_details.get("email")

        if email is None or email.strip() == "":
            # Missing email address, this should always be present as the data
            # privacy team verify this in OneTrust before the request reaches
            # our system

            logger.warning(
                "Request %s has no email so will be transitioned to need-more-detail",
                request.id,
            )
            # Update the request before raising an exception
            self._transition_request(request, "need-more-detail", "no email address")
            raise MissingEmailAddress(request)  # TODO: Should we raise?

        # TODO: Update OneTrust in some way to tell it that we've started to
        # process a given request

        self._transition_request(request, "in-progress")

    def process_in_progress(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self._validate_request("in-progress", request)

        # TODO: In the original process this "in-progress" stage did not do a
        # great deal

        # Enrich the request if possible or required
        self.enrich_request(request)

        # TODO: Update OneTrust in some way to tell it that we've moved to the
        # next phase

        # Move to the next state
        self._transition_request(request, "phase-1")

    def process_phase1(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self._validate_request("phase-1", request)
        logger.info("Running request %s through Phase-1", request.id)
        # This is the legacy system stage
        # For now all this should do is notify the AS400 team via both Slack
        # AND Microsoft Teams
        self.notify_as400_team(request)

        # TODO: Update OneTrust that phase-1 was done

        self._transition_request(request, "phase-2")

    def process_phase2(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        # This is where the core of erasure logic happened in Tines
        self._validate_request("phase-2", request)

        # TODO: Update OneTrust throughout each part of phase-2

        # TODO: Subflow 1 from Tines:
        # TODO: Search for the customer in All Sites using the Customer API
        # TODO: Submit an erasure to the Customer Erasures API
        # If we found the sites for a customer we include those, otherwise it
        # is not included

        # TODO: Subflow 2 from Tines:
        # TODO: Check the Customer Erasures API Status for the Erasure Request
        # If it's successful we then transition to the next subflow

        # TODO: Subflow 3 from Tines:
        # TODO: Search and Delete Zendesk Tickets

        # TODO: Subflow 4 from Tines:
        # TODO: Search and delete Zendesk users

        # TODO: Subflow 5 from Tines:
        # TODO: Purge the deleted Zendesk tickets

        # TODO: Subflow 6 from Tines:
        # TODO: Purge the deleted Zendesk users

        # Finally after all this we transition to the next stage

        raise NotImplementedError("phase-2 Request processing not implemented")

    def process_phase3(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self._validate_request("phase-3", request)
        # TODO: Phase-3 from Tines
        # This is just checking if the customer still appears in the customer
        # API or not
        # TODO: What should we do if the previous erasure states all failed?

        # Finally transition to done
        raise NotImplementedError("phase-3 Request processing not implemented")

    def enrich_request(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        """Enrich the erasure request if needed with details from other sources"""
        # Enrichment could include getting more details on the customer that
        # will be needed by downstream erasure processes

        if "email" not in request.erasure_details:
            raise MissingEmailAddress(request)

        # We will try and find details from all customer regions
        enrichment_data: list[CustomerDetails] = []
        enrichment_data.extend(
            self.customer_api.get_customer_details(
                email=request.erasure_details["email"]
            )
        )
        for site_id in ALL_SITE_IDS:
            enrichment_data.extend(
                self.customer_api.get_customer_details(
                    email=request.erasure_details["email"], site_id=site_id
                )
            )

        for entry in enrichment_data:
            # Enrich with the first found entries
            # If the customer has used the email in multiple regions with
            # different details this might enrich with weird values.
            # Essentially this is a best effort enrichment as email should be
            # enough for most systems to erase by, the extra details are just
            # useful for debugging purposes.
            if entry.first_name and "first-name" not in request.erasure_details:
                request.erasure_details["first-name"] = entry.first_name
            if entry.last_name and "last-name" not in request.erasure_details:
                request.erasure_details["last-name"] = entry.last_name
            if entry.loyalty_number and "rfl" not in request.erasure_details:
                request.erasure_details["rfl"] = entry.loyalty_number
            if entry.mobile_number and "phone-number" not in request.erasure_details:
                request.erasure_details["phone-number"] = entry.mobile_number

    def notify_as400_team(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        logger.info("Notifying AS400 of request %s", request.id)
        self.as400.notify(request)

    def send_status_update(self, request: ErasureRequest, update: str) -> None:
        """Send a status update to OneTrust"""
        logger.info("Sending update to OneTrust for %s: %s", request.id, update)
        raise NotImplementedError("Sending status update to OneTrust not implemented")


class AllProcessingFailed(Exception):

    def __init__(self, count: int) -> None:
        self.count: int = count
        super().__init__(f"Processing all {count} requests failed")


class ErasureRequestException(Exception, ABC):
    """Parent exception for Erasure Request process"""

    def __init__(self, request: ErasureRequest, message: str) -> None:
        self.request: ErasureRequest = request
        super().__init__(message)


class InvalidRequestState(ErasureRequestException):
    """Exception raised when a request is not in a valid state"""

    def __init__(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self.state: str = request.state
        super().__init__(
            request=request,
            message=f"Request {request.id} is in an invalid state: {self.state}",
        )


class WrongStateForProcess(ErasureRequestException):
    """Exception raised when a request is in the wrong state for the given process."""

    def __init__(self, request: ErasureRequest, expected: ErasureState) -> None:
        self.state: str = request.state
        super().__init__(
            request=request,
            message=f"Invalid state on request {request.id} for process: {self.state} != {expected}",
        )


class MissingEmailAddress(ErasureRequestException):
    """Exception raised if the request is missing an email address"""

    def __init__(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        super().__init__(
            request=request, message=f"Missing email address on request {request.id}"
        )

1	import base64
2	import json
3	from datetime import datetime
4	from typing import Final, cast
5	
6	from aws_lambda_powertools import Logger
7	from aws_lambda_powertools.tracing import Tracer
8	from aws_lambda_powertools.utilities.typing import LambdaContext
9	from dotenv import load_dotenv
10	
11	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging import AuditLogger, get_audit_logger
12	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.dryrun import is_dry_run
13	from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import (
14	    VALID_DETAIL_KEYS,
15	    ErasureDetailKey,
16	    ErasureRequest,
17	)
18	
19	from .request_repository import RequestRepository, get_request_repository
20	
21	# Load .env file, mostly useful for testing
22	_ = load_dotenv()
23	
24	logger = Logger()
25	tracer = Tracer()  # Sets service via POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME env var
26	
27	in_dry_run_mode: Final[bool] = is_dry_run()
28	request_repo: RequestRepository = get_request_repository()
29	audit: AuditLogger = get_audit_logger()
30	
31	
32	class InvalidRequestError(ValueError):
33	    pass
34	
35	
36	_MAX_FIELD_LEN = 512
37	
38	
39	def _require_str(body: dict[str, object], key: str) -> str:
40	    v = body[key]
41	    if not isinstance(v, str) or not v:
42	        raise InvalidRequestError(f"Field '{key}' must be a non-empty string")
43	    if len(v) > _MAX_FIELD_LEN:
44	        raise InvalidRequestError(
45	            f"Field '{key}' must be <= {_MAX_FIELD_LEN} characters"
46	        )
47	    return v
48	
49	
50	def request_from_event(
51	    event: dict[str, object],
52	) -> ErasureRequest:
53	    """Convert the input event into an ErasureRequest"""
54	    try:
55	        raw_body: str = cast(str, event.get("body") or "")
56	        if event.get("isBase64Encoded"):
57	            raw_body = base64.b64decode(raw_body).decode("utf-8")
58	        body: dict[str, object] = cast(dict[str, object], json.loads(raw_body))
59	    except (json.JSONDecodeError, ValueError) as e:
60	        raise InvalidRequestError(f"Invalid JSON body: {e}") from e
61	
62	    required_fields = ("id", "source", "date_issued", "erasure_details")
63	    missing = [f for f in required_fields if f not in body]
64	    if missing:
65	        raise InvalidRequestError(f"Missing required fields: {', '.join(missing)}")
66	
67	    state_raw = body.get("state", "new")
68	    if not isinstance(state_raw, str):
69	        raise InvalidRequestError("Field 'state' must be a string")
70	    if state_raw != "new":
71	        raise InvalidRequestError(
72	            f"Erasure requests must be created in state 'new', got '{state_raw}'"
73	        )
74	
75	    date_issued_raw = body["date_issued"]
76	    if not isinstance(date_issued_raw, str):
77	        raise InvalidRequestError("Field 'date_issued' must be a string")
78	    try:
79	        date_issued = datetime.fromisoformat(date_issued_raw)
80	    except ValueError as e:
81	        raise InvalidRequestError(f"Invalid date_issued: {e}") from e
82	
83	    id_ = _require_str(body, "id")
84	    source = _require_str(body, "source")
85	
86	    erasure_details_raw = body["erasure_details"]
87	    if not isinstance(erasure_details_raw, dict):
88	        raise InvalidRequestError("erasure_details must be an object")
89	    erasure_details: dict[ErasureDetailKey, str] = {}
90	    for k, v in cast(dict[str, object], erasure_details_raw).items():
91	        if k not in VALID_DETAIL_KEYS:
92	            raise InvalidRequestError(f"Unknown erasure_details key: '{k}'")
93	        if not isinstance(v, str) or not v:
94	            raise InvalidRequestError(
95	                f"erasure_details['{k}'] must be a non-empty string"
96	            )
97	        erasure_details[k] = v  # type: ignore[literal-required]
98	
99	    return ErasureRequest(
100	        id=id_,
101	        source=source,
102	        state=state_raw,
103	        date_issued=date_issued,
104	        erasure_details=erasure_details,
105	    )
106	
107	
108	def _response(
109	    status_code: int,
110	    body: dict[str, str | int],
111	) -> dict[str, object]:
112	    return {
113	        "statusCode": status_code,
114	        "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
115	        "body": json.dumps(body),
116	    }
117	
118	
119	@tracer.capture_lambda_handler
120	@logger.inject_lambda_context
121	def lambda_handler(
122	    event: dict[str, object],
123	    _context: LambdaContext,
124	) -> dict[str, object]:
125	    """
126	    Entrypoint for AWS Lambda that does item creation.
127	
128	    This should be very simple, the Lambda should simply create an entry in the
129	    underlying storage, another lambda or piece of compute will process them.
130	    """
131	    try:
132	        request: ErasureRequest = request_from_event(event)
133	    except (InvalidRequestError, json.JSONDecodeError) as e:
134	        logger.warning("Rejecting erasure request with invalid body: %s", str(e))
135	        return _response(400, {"error": "invalid request body"})
136	
137	    try:
138	        if in_dry_run_mode:
139	            logger.warning("DryRun: Would have created request for %s", request.id)
140	        else:
141	            request_repo.create(request)
142	    except Exception:
143	        logger.exception("Failed to create erasure request %s", str(request))
144	        return _response(500, {"error": "internal error"})
145	
146	    audit.log(request.id, "Erasure request was created")
147	    return _response(200, {"id": request.id})
148

import os
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from typing import Any, Final, Literal

from aws_lambda_powertools import Logger
from aws_lambda_powertools.tracing import Tracer
from aws_lambda_powertools.utilities.typing import LambdaContext
from dotenv import load_dotenv

from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.as400 import AS400Notifier, NoOpAS400Notifier
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging import AuditLogger, get_audit_logger
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.customer_api_client import (
    CustomerAPIClient,
    HTTPCustomerAPIClient,
)
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.dryrun import is_dry_run
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_processor import RequestProcessor
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_repository import (
    RequestRepository,
    get_request_repository,
)

SAFETY_MARGIN_MS_ENV_VAR: Literal["PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS"] = (
    "PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS"
)
DEFAULT_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS: Literal[1000] = 1000

CUSTOMER_API_URL_ENV_VAR: Literal["CUSTOMER_API_URL"] = "CUSTOMER_API_URL"


def get_customer_api_url() -> str:
    """Get the Customer API URL from the environment, throwing a ValueError if not found"""
    url = os.getenv(CUSTOMER_API_URL_ENV_VAR)
    if url is None:
        raise ValueError(f"{CUSTOMER_API_URL_ENV_VAR} is not defined")
    return url


# Load .env file, mostly useful for testing
_ = load_dotenv()

logger = Logger()
tracer = Tracer()  # Sets service via POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME env var

customer_api_url: str = get_customer_api_url()
in_dry_run_mode: Final[bool] = is_dry_run()
request_repo: RequestRepository = get_request_repository()
audit: AuditLogger = get_audit_logger()
as400: AS400Notifier = NoOpAS400Notifier()  # TODO: add actual AS400 notifying
customer_api: CustomerAPIClient = HTTPCustomerAPIClient(customer_api_url)
processor: RequestProcessor = RequestProcessor(
    request_repo, audit, as400, customer_api, dry_run=in_dry_run_mode
)


def get_safety_margin_ms() -> int:
    """Get the safety margin in milliseconds from the environment"""
    from_env = os.getenv("PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS")
    safety_margin_ms: int = DEFAULT_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS
    if from_env is not None:
        try:
            safety_margin_ms = int(from_env)
        except ValueError:
            logger.error(
                "Could not parse %s value %s as an integer",
                SAFETY_MARGIN_MS_ENV_VAR,
                from_env,
            )
    return safety_margin_ms


def get_timeout_datetime(remaining_millis: int) -> datetime:
    """Convert a remaining millis into a datetime"""
    delta = timedelta(milliseconds=remaining_millis)
    return datetime.now() + delta


@tracer.capture_lambda_handler
@logger.inject_lambda_context
def lambda_handler(
    _event: dict[Any, Any],  # pyright: ignore[reportExplicitAny]
    context: LambdaContext,
) -> None:
    """
    Entrypoint for AWS Lambda that does erasure request processing.
    """

    # Add a margin for safety so Lambdas don't timeout mid process
    safety_margin_ms: int = get_safety_margin_ms()

    remaining_millis = context.get_remaining_time_in_millis() - safety_margin_ms
    timeout: datetime = get_timeout_datetime(remaining_millis)
    logger.info(
        f"Processing events with a timeout of {remaining_millis}ms until {timeout}"
    )

    # Actual processing logic should be isolated in the processor class
    processor.process(timeout)

from dataclasses import dataclass, fields
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Literal, override

type ErasureState = Literal[
    "new", "in-progress", "phase-1", "phase-2", "phase-3", "done", "need-more-detail"
]
"""Valid erasure states type"""

type ErasureDetailKey = Literal[
    "first-name", "last-name", "email", "postcode", "phone-number", "rfl", "last-order"
]
"""Expected erasure details keys"""

VALID_DETAIL_KEYS: frozenset[ErasureDetailKey] = frozenset(
    (
        "first-name",
        "last-name",
        "email",
        "postcode",
        "phone-number",
        "rfl",
        "last-order",
    )
)
"""Valid keys for erasure_details"""

VALID_STATES: tuple[ErasureState, ...] = (
    "new",
    "in-progress",
    "phase-1",
    "phase-2",
    "phase-3",
    "done",
    "need-more-detail",
)
"""Valid erasure states"""


@dataclass
class ErasureRequest:
    """
    An Erasure request as submitted by a source system.

    The normal source system is OneTrust.
    """

    id: str
    """
    A unique identifier for this erasure request.
    This should come from the source system.
    """

    source: str
    """The source of this Erasure request"""

    state: ErasureState
    """The state that the request is in"""

    date_issued: datetime
    """The date (and time) that this request was issued at"""

    # TODO: Perhaps turn erasure_details into its own domain object
    erasure_details: dict[ErasureDetailKey, str]
    """
    Specific details for this erasure.

    This is a sensitive field as it will contain the likes of email addresses,
    addresses etc.

    The erasure details we normally get from OneTrust are:

    - First Name
    - Last Name
    - Email
    - Postcode

    With the optional ones being:

    - Phone Number
    - RFL Number
    - Last Order Number
    """

    # Below code attempts to omit the erasure_details from inclusing in strings
    # by mistake

    def safe_dict(self) -> dict[str, str]:
        """
        Returns a dictionary version of the erasure request with personal
        details masked.
        """
        return {
            f.name: getattr(self, f.name)
            for f in fields(self)
            if f.name not in ["erasure_details"]
        }

    def unsafe_dict(self) -> dict[str, str | dict[str, str]]:
        """
        Returns a dictionary version of the erasure request with personal
        details present.
        """
        return {f.name: getattr(self, f.name) for f in fields(self)}

    @override
    def __str__(self):
        d = self.safe_dict()
        return f"ErasureRequest(id={d['id']}, source={d['source']}, state={d['state']}, date_issued={d['date_issued']})"

    @override
    def __repr__(self):
        d = self.safe_dict()
        return f"ErasureRequest(**{d})"

    @property
    def date_issued_ms(self) -> int:
        """The date and time issued as a unix timestamp (milliseconds)"""
        timestamp_ms = int(self.date_issued.timestamp() * 1000)
        return timestamp_ms
